one_line_takeaway: AgentAuditor is a training-free, retrieval-plus-generated-CoT memory wrapper that pushes generic LLM judges to human-level accuracy at auditing LLM-agent safety and security, paired with ASSEBench, a 2,293-record benchmark that jointly covers both safety and security evaluation.
key_finding: Adding AgentAuditor consistently and often dramatically raises evaluator accuracy across all 10 models and all datasets, and Gemini-2.0-Flash-thinking + AgentAuditor reaches human-annotator-level performance. Gemini-2+AA hits 96.31 F1 / 96.10 Acc on R-Judge (vs 82.27/81.21 origin) and 93.17 F1 / 93.15 Acc on ASSEBench-Security; on R-Judge it beats the fine-tuned ShieldAgent by 15.1%. Weak models benefit most, e.g. Llama-3.1-8B accuracy on ASSEBench-Security jumps 49.69 -> 81.03. The ablation shows strong component interdependence: CoT-only (81.03 F1/77.84 Acc) or Few-shot-only actually underperform the raw base model (82.27 F1/81.21 Acc), yet the full system reaches 96.31 F1/96.10 Acc - retrieval-selected exemplars plus validated CoT are jointly necessary. The Strict/Lenient subsets show AgentAuditor adaptively shrinks a model's cross-standard gap (Gemini-2 gap 13.2% -> -2.8%).
core_contribution: The paper makes two coupled contributions of its own (not merely cited prior work). (1) AgentAuditor, a universal, training-free, memory-augmented reasoning framework that turns an off-the-shelf LLM into a stronger 'LLM-as-a-judge' evaluator of agent safety/security. It runs one single LLM across three stages: Feature Memory Construction (the LLM adaptively extracts structured semantic features - scenario s, risk type r, behavior mode b - from raw interaction records and embeds them with Nomic-Embed-Text-v1.5), Reasoning Memory Construction (L2-normalize + weight + PCA-reduce the feature vectors, cluster them with FINCH selecting ~10% of the dataset as representative 'shots', then have the LLM generate a ground-truth-conditioned chain-of-thought for each shot), and RAG-based Memory-Augmented Reasoning (a two-stage retrieval: Top-n by content-embedding cosine similarity, then re-rank by weighted feature-embedding similarity to pick k CoT exemplars that are injected as a few-shot CoT prompt for the target case). (2) ASSEBench, described as the first benchmark built specifically to measure how well LLM-based evaluators detect both agent safety risks and agent security threats. Note: the record's 'abstract' field was garbled/truncated; this review is written from the full PDF, not that fragment.
threat_model: The paper's object of study is the reliability of automated evaluators of LLM-agent behavior, not a direct attacker-vs-system defense. It explicitly distinguishes Agent Safety (unintended harmful behavior from internal factors - flawed design, limited risk awareness, e.g. unintentional information leakage or autonomously executing property-damaging actions) from Agent Security (protecting agents from external deliberate attacks such as prompt injection, data poisoning, and backdoor attacks). The security threats appear as content inside the evaluated interaction records (e.g. the worked example shows a prompt-injection 'Ignore previous instructions...' attack whose goal is achieved). AgentAuditor itself is a measurement/audit tool: the failure it targets is that rule-based and LLM-based evaluators miss step-by-step dangers, subtle meaning, compounding risk, and ambiguous safety rules, yielding unreliable safety scores (ASR, refusal rate, etc.).
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: effectiveness depends on the label quality of reasoning-memory shots (defective labels reduce gains); it inherits CoT-method costs (needs a model with reasoning ability, higher resource/compute consumption); it relies on several heuristic parameters (weights, N=8/K=3, 10% cluster target) whose universality and interpretability are unverified; although the method theoretically supports non-binary labels, both ASSEBench and all tests use only binary safe/unsafe labels; and ASSEBench is entirely English, limiting multilingual coverage. Reviewer-added caveats: gains are largest precisely where base models are weakest and headroom is largest; 'human-level' is benchmarked against the average of a single human annotator, not an expert consensus; and a portion of the test set is consumed to build the CoT memory (excluded from scoring, but it still requires labeled in-domain data).

datasets_used: ASSEBench (this paper's own benchmark): 2,293 annotated interaction records, 15 risk types, 29 application scenarios, 528 interaction environments, 26 behavior modes; 4 subsets - ASSEBench-Security (817 records), ASSEBench-Safety / ASSEBench-Strict / ASSEBench-Lenient (1,476 records each)
R-Judge (existing benchmark, used for evaluation and ablation)
AgentHarm (used for evaluation and as a source benchmark)

Source benchmarks reproduced to build ASSEBench: AgentSafetyBench, AgentSecurityBench, AgentDojo, AgentHarm (initial pool 13,587 records reduced to 4,527 via 'Balanced Allocation'; records generated with Gemini-2.0-Flash-thinking, GPT-4o, Claude-3.5-sonnet)
quantitative_results: Gemini-2 + AgentAuditor on R-Judge: 96.31 F1 / 96.10 Acc (origin 82.27 / 81.21)
Gemini-2 + AgentAuditor on ASSEBench-Security: 93.17 F1 / 93.15 Acc (origin 67.25 / 72.34)
Gemini-2+AA beats fine-tuned ShieldAgent on R-Judge by 15.1%
Human-level claim: AgentAuditor Acc 96.1% (R-Judge), 99.4% (AgentHarm), 93.2% (ASSEBench-Security) vs single-human-annotator Acc 95.7%, 99.3%, 94.3%

Ablation (Gemini-2, R-Judge): full system 96.31 F1 / 96.31 Recall / 96.10 Acc; raw base model 82.27 / 82.55 / 81.21; CoT-only 81.03 / 89.60 / 77.84; Feature-Tagging+Clustering+Few-shot without CoT 83.74 / 81.21 / 83.33
Fixed hyperparameters: N=8, K=3, embedding dimension 512
GPT-4o F1 gain on ASSEBench-Security: +67.7% (50.98 -> 85.48), the largest single reported F1 improvement
baselines_compared: Base LLMs evaluated with vs without AgentAuditor: Gemini-2.0-Flash-thinking, Deepseek V3, GPT-o3-mini, GPT-4.1, GPT-4o, Claude-3.5-sonnet, QwQ-32B, Qwen-2.5-32B, Qwen-2.5-7B, Llama-3.1-8B
Fine-tuned specialized evaluators: ShieldAgent (fine-tuned from Qwen-2.5-7B), Llama-Guard-3 (fine-tuned from Llama-3.1-8B)
Ablation baselines / variants: raw base model (no components), CoT-only (Auto-CoT-style), Few-shot Top-K, Few-shot Random, all-samples few-shot
